blocks.body: Dependent Prepositions helps you remember fixed patterns such as good at, listen to, and reason for as complete combinations.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"Dependent prepositions are fixed combinations in which an adjective, verb, or noun regularly takes a particular preposition.","rules":[{"label":"Adjective + preposition","color":"emerald","text":"Many adjectives have a usual partner: good at, afraid of, interested in.","example":"She is interested in design."},{"label":"Verb + preposition","color":"blue","text":"Some verbs also need a specific preposition: listen to, depend on, wait for.","example":"We are waiting for the bus."},{"label":"Noun + preposition","color":"amber","text":"Nouns often keep the same pattern: reason for, answer to, increase in.","example":"There was a rise in prices."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. Core idea and form
blocks.body:
With dependent prepositions, it is usually better not to search for one universal rule, but to learn the whole expression.

  • Adjective + preposition: famous for, ready for, proud of, worried about.
  • Verb + preposition: belong to, suffer from, agree with, deal with.
  • Noun + preposition: need for, link between, invitation to, experience in.

The preposition often helps you predict what comes next: an object, a noun phrase, or an -ing form.

blocks.heading: 2. Main uses and examples
blocks.body:
PatternExamplesUse
adjective + prepositiongood at maths, afraid of dogs, interested in musicdescribing ability, emotion, or attitude
verb + prepositionlisten to a podcast, depend on your team, wait for a replyshowing an action plus its target
noun + prepositionreason for the change, answer to the question, increase in demandnaming a relationship around a noun

If you are unsure, check not only the word but the full pattern in a dictionary or a reliable example.

blocks.heading: 3. Common mistakes
blocks.body:
• Mistake: translating directly from your first language.
    Better: learn interested in as a unit instead of inventing the preposition.
  • Mistake: depend of.
    Better: depend on.
  • Mistake: married with in the general sense.
    Better: married to.
  • Mistake: adding an unnecessary preposition after a verb that does not need one: discuss the plan, not discuss about the plan.

blocks.heading: 4. Quick rule
blocks.body:
• Learn word + preposition as one block.
  • Check whether the pattern is followed by a noun, pronoun, or -ing form.
  • Do not rely on literal translation, because it often fails in fixed combinations.
